content:
Key Findings

  • Preston City Council paid 2,517 unique suppliers between 2019/20 and 2025/26
  • 585 suppliers received under £1,000 each, accounting for 23.2% of all suppliers
  • Only 23 suppliers received over £1 million each, representing 0.9% of all suppliers
  • 1,036 suppliers (41.2% of the total) received between £1,000 and £10,000 each
  • 677 suppliers (26.9% of the total) received between £10,000 and £100,000 each

The Long Tail of Preston's Suppliers

Preston City Council's spending data reveals a long tail of suppliers, with the vast majority receiving relatively small amounts. Out of 2,517 unique suppliers, 585 received under £1,000 each. This group represents 23.2% of all suppliers but likely accounts for a tiny fraction of the total £205.9 million spent over seven years. The sheer number of small transactions suggests significant administrative overhead in managing these relationships.

The long tail phenomenon is not unique to Preston, but the scale is notable. With 2,517 suppliers, the council must manage contracts, invoices, and relationships for a vast network of businesses. The question arises: could consolidation save money and reduce administrative burden? While small suppliers often provide specialised or local services, streamlining procurement could free up resources for other priorities.

Supplier Size Distribution

The distribution of supplier sizes shows a clear hierarchy. At the top, 23 suppliers received over £1 million each, representing just 0.9% of all suppliers. These large suppliers likely provide essential services or goods that require significant investment. In contrast, the 585 suppliers who received under £1,000 each make up 23.2% of the total, highlighting the fragmented nature of Preston's procurement.

The middle ground is also significant. 1,036 suppliers (41.2% of the total) received between £1,000 and £10,000 each, while 677 suppliers (26.9%) received between £10,000 and £100,000. This distribution suggests a diverse range of services and goods, from one-off purchases to ongoing contracts.

Supplier Size Number of Suppliers Percentage of Total
Under £1,000 585 23.2%
£1,000 to £10,000 1,036 41.2%
£10,000 to £100,000 677 26.9%
£100,000 to £1 million 196 7.8%
Over £1 million 23 0.9%

Potential for Consolidation

Consolidating procurement could reduce administrative costs and improve efficiency. However, it's essential to balance the benefits of economies of scale with the need for diverse, local suppliers. Preston's long tail of suppliers may include many small businesses that provide specialised or niche services. Consolidating too aggressively could risk losing these services or harming local economic development.

One approach could be to identify areas where consolidation is possible without sacrificing quality or diversity. For example, standardising certain goods or services across departments could reduce the number of suppliers while maintaining service levels. Alternatively, the council could explore frameworks or dynamic purchasing systems that allow for both consolidation and flexibility.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Preston City Council about their procurement consolidation strategies
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about procurement policies and decisions
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see which suppliers are awarded contracts and for what services
  • Write to your local councillor about the potential benefits and risks of consolidating procurement
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and analyse supplier relationships yourself
This article is based on Preston City Council's published spending data covering 2019/20 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
